---
title: "European Frog-Bit (Hydrocharis morsus-ranae)"
slug: "hydrocharis-morsus-ranae"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/356044502/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "European Frog-Bit"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Alismatales"
tax_family: "Hydrocharitaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Alismatales", "Hydrocharitaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/356044502/medium.jpeg" alt="European Frog-Bit" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/mmcmillian">
mmcmillian</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/201674680">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Alismatales
-   **Family:** Hydrocharitaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=81600)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida (virtuyally non-existant) found in still or slow-moving freshwater habitats.

## Natural history notes

A small, free-floating aquatic plant characterized by its round, glossy leaves and small white flowers with three petals. In Florida, it is occasionally found in still or slow-moving freshwater habitats such as ponds, canals, and marshes, though it is not widespread. It reproduces vegetatively and can form dense floating mats under suitable conditions.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/81600-Hydrocharis-morsus-ranae)
-   [Local profile](https://plant-directory.ifas.ufl.edu/plant-directory/hydrocharis-morsus-ranae/)
